perturbation.original_text: (7) that in the event any proceeding to enforce this Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement or decide any matter relating to it Recipient becomes necessary, the Agreement shall be governed by the laws of Switzerland pertaining thereto and the Recipient agrees to submit to the non-exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the Canton of Geneva, Switzerland
perturbation.changed_text: (7) that in the event any proceeding to enforce this Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement or decide any matter relating to it Recipient becomes necessary, the Agreement shall be governed by the laws of Switzerland pertaining thereto and the Recipient agrees to submit to the exclusive jurisdiction of the courts of the Canton of Geneva, Switzerland
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'non-exclusive' jurisdiction to 'exclusive' jurisdiction may conflict with laws regarding jurisdiction and venue, especially if the Recipient is based in a different country and would otherwise have grounds to litigate in their home jurisdiction.
perturbation.contradicted_law: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs
perturbation.law_explanation: By mandating exclusive jurisdiction in Switzerland, the modified text potentially deprives a U.S. citizen of their right to access U.S. federal courts based on diversity of citizenship, where the amount in controversy exceeds $75,000. While forum selection clauses are generally enforceable, they can be challenged if they effectively deprive a party of their legal rights.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs | U.S. Code | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Quick search by citation: Title Section Go! 28 U.S. Code § 1332 - Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs U.S. Code Notes prev | next (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between— (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state, except that the district courts shall not have original jurisdiction under this subsection of an action between citizens of a State and citizens or subjects of a foreign state who are lawfully admitted for permanent residence in the United States and are domiciled in the same State; (3) citizens of different States and in which citizens or subjects of a foreign state are additional parties; and (4) a foreign state, defined in section 1603(a) of this title , as plaintiff and citizens of a State or of different States. (b) Except when express provision therefor is otherwise made in a statute of the United States, where the plaintiff who files the case originally in the Federal courts is finally adjudged to be entitled to recover less than the sum or value of $75,000, computed without regard to any setoff or counterclaim to which the defendant may be adjudged to be entitled, and exclusive of interest and costs, the district court may deny costs to the plaintiff and, in addition, may impose costs on the plaintiff. (c) For the purposes of this section and section 1441 of this title — (1) a corporation shall be deemed to be a citizen of every State and foreign state by which it has been incorporated and of the State or foreign state where it has its principal place of business, except that in any direct action against the insurer of a policy or contract of liability insura

Diversity of citizenship; amount in controversy; costs (a) The district courts shall have original jurisdiction of all civil actions where the matter in controversy exceeds the sum or value of $75,000, exclusive of interest and costs, and is between- (1) citizens of different States; (2) citizens of a State and citizens or subjects o
perturbation.justification: The change from 'non-exclusive' to 'exclusive' jurisdiction in the contract directly contradicts the potential right of a U.S. citizen to access U.S. federal courts based on diversity of citizenship, as explained in the Law Explanation. Specifically, 28 U.S. Code § 1332 grants district courts original jurisdiction over civil actions exceeding $75,000 between citizens of different states or between citizens of a state and citizens of a foreign state. The modified text aims to remove the right to litigate somewhere else, directly conflicting with their right to have jurisdiction outside of Switzerland.

perturbation.explanation: Changing the date to 2025 makes the contract appear to be signed in the future. Since the original context states this agreement was entered in 2015, it is a contradiction.
perturbation.law_explanation: Fundamental contract law requires a 'meeting of the minds' and present intent to enter into an agreement. A signature date in the future indicates that the agreement was not in effect at the time the parties purportedly agreed to it. This creates ambiguity concerning the contract's validity and timing of obligations, which is a challenge to standard principles requiring definite and certain agreement terms.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: contract | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you contract A contract is an agreement between parties , creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law . The basic elements required for the agreement to be a legally enforceable contract are: mutual assent , expressed by a valid offer and acceptance ; adequate consideration ; capacity ; and legality . In some states , elements of consideration can be satisfied by a valid substitute. Possible remedies for breach of contract include general damages , consequential damages , reliance damages , and specific performance . Background : Contracts are promises that the law will enforce. Contract law is generally governed by state common law , and while general overall contract law is common throughout the country, some specific court interpretations of a particular element of the contract may vary between the states. If a promise is breached, the law provides remedies to the harmed party, often in the form of monetary damages, or in limited circumstances, in the form of specific performance of the promise made. Elements -- Consideration and Mutual Assent Contracts arise when a duty comes into existence, because of a promise made by one of the parties. To be legally binding as a contract, a promise must be exchanged for adequate consideration. There are two different theories or definitions of consideration: Bargain Theory of Consideration and Benefit-Detriment theory of consideration. Benefit-Detriment Under the b enefit-detriment theory, an adequate consideration exists only when a promise made to the benefit of the promisor or to the detriment of the promisee, which reasonably and fairly induces the promisor to make a promise for something else for the promisee. For example, promises that are purely gifts are not considered enforceable because the personal satisfaction the grantor of the promise may receive from the act of generosity is normally not considered sufficient detriment to constitute adequate consideration. Bargain-for-Exchange Under Bargain-for-Exchange theory of consideration, adequate consideration exists when a promisor makes a promise in return for something else. Here, the essential condition is that the promisor was given something specifically to induce the promise being made. In other words, the bargain for exchange theory is different from the detriment-benefit theory in that the focus in bargain for exchange theory seems to be the parties’ motive for making the promises and the parties’ subjective mutual assent, while in detriment benefit theory, the focus seems to be an objective legal detriment or benefit to the parties. Governing Laws Contracts are mainly governed by state statutory and common (judge-made) law and private law (i.e. the private agreement). Private law principally includes the terms of the agreement between the parties who are exchanging promises. This private law may override many of the rules otherwise established by state law. Statutory law, such as the Statute of Fraud , may require some kinds of contracts be put in writing and executed with particular formalities, for the contract to be enforceable. Otherwise, the parties may enter into a binding agreement without signing a formal written document. For example, Virginia Supreme Court has held in Lucy v. Zehmer that even an agreement made on a piece of napkin can be considered a valid contract, if the parties were both sane, and showed mutual assent and consideration. Most of the principles of the common law of contracts are outlined in the Restatement of Law, Second Contracts published by the American Law Institute. The Uniform Commercial Code , whose original articles have been adopted in nearly every state, represents a body of statutory law that governs important categories of contracts. The main articles that deal with the law of contracts are Article 1 (General Provisions) and Article 2 (Sales) . Sections of Article 9 (
perturbation.justification: The changed text modifies the contract's signature date to 2025, while the original context implies the agreement was made in 2015. This creates an inconsistency regarding the timing of the agreement. The Law Explanation states that contract law requires 'mutual assent' and 'present intent,' and a future signature date indicates the agreement was not in effect when purportedly agreed upon. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines a contract as 'an agreement between parties, creating mutual obligations that are enforceable by law' and requires 'mutual assent.' The future date contradicts the concept of 'present intent' and 'mutual assent' at the time of signing, thus undermining the contract's enforceability. This falls under the 'Inconsistencies Legal Contradiction' definition, where a time-sensitive element (the signature date) does not align, making the contractual terms unenforceable.

perturbation.original_text: (8) that continued adherence to this Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement is an essential term of employment as a Staff Member of the Association and/or an essential part of the Recipient’s roles and responsibilities as a Director or Officer, the violation of which will permit the Association to terminate such employment or terminate such person from his7her position in addition to seeking any other legal or equitable remedies.
perturbation.changed_text: (8) that continued adherence to this Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement is an essential term of employment as a Staff Member of the Association and/or an essential part of the Recipient’s roles and responsibilities as a Director or Officer, the violation of which will automatically result in termination from such employment or termination from his/her position.
perturbation.explanation: Changing 'will permit the Association to terminate' to 'will automatically result in termination' creates a scenario where the employee is automatically terminated for any breach of the NDA. This could be seen as too strict and in conflict with just cause termination laws.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Wrongful Termination Laws - Switzerland Code of Obligations Art. 337
perturbation.law_explanation: Swiss law (Art. 337 of the Code of Obligations) requires 'just cause' for immediate termination. A minor breach of an NDA, depending on the circumstances, may not constitute just cause. Enforcing automatic termination for *any* NDA violation could be deemed a wrongful termination if a court finds the breach insufficient to warrant immediate dismissal. Automatic termination also bypasses any progressive discipline procedures that may be required.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Fedlex DE: Vielen Dank, dass Sie sie die Website des Bundesrechts aufgerufen haben; sie ist nur mit einem Javascript-fähigen Browser verfügbar. Um diese Website optimal nutzen zu können, empfehlen wir Ihnen, einen aktuellen Browser zu verwenden. FR: Merci d'avoir rejoint le site du droit fédéral; celui-ci n'est disponible qu'avec un navigateur supportant javascript. Afin de profiter d'une expérience maximale sur ce site, nous vous conseillons d'utiliser un navigateur récent. IT: Grazie per aver scelto il sito web del diritto federale; questo è disponibile soltanto con un browser che supporta JavaScript. Per navigare in modo ottimale in questo sito, vi consigliamo di utilizzare un browser più recente. RM: Grazia fitg che Vus visitais la pagina d’internet dal dretg federal; ella è disponibla mo cun in navigatur che sustegna javascript. Per pudair far diever da questa pagina d’internet en moda optimala As recumandain nus d’utilisar in navigatur actual. In order to be able to use all the features of this site, we recommend that you use an up-to-date browser. In order to be able to use all the features of this site, we recommend that you use an up-to-date browser.
perturbation.justification: The original text allows the Association to terminate employment for violations of the Confidentiality and Non-Disclosure Agreement, while the changed text mandates automatic termination for any violation. The Law Explanation suggests this could conflict with 'just cause' termination laws, implying that not all NDA breaches warrant immediate dismissal. The change from 'will permit' to 'will automatically result in' creates a significant shift that could be deemed wrongful termination under Swiss law (Art. 337 of the Code of Obligations). While Scraped Law Snippet 1 discusses browser compatibility for a federal law website, and Snippet 2 is missing, the Law Explanation provided, combined with the text modification, suggests a violation of just cause termination principles by mandating automatic termination without consideration of the severity of the breach.

perturbation.original_text: Primary Purpose The Primary Purpose of the Agreement is to enable the sharing of information between the Association and the Recipient in a manner that enables the proper functioning of the Association and the full participation of members while also providing access to Confidential Information (as defined in this Agreement) and the responsibilities to ensure that such information is not disseminated or used inappropriately.
perturbation.changed_text: Primary Purpose The Primary Purpose of the Agreement is to enable the sharing of information between the Association and the Recipient in a manner that enables the proper functioning of the Association and the full participation of members. The recipient is not responsible to ensure that such information is not disseminated or used inappropriately.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the 'responsibilities to ensure that such information is not disseminated or used inappropriately' could create a conflict with Swiss data protection laws, by creating a contract that seemingly waives recipient responsibility for data misuse.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Swiss Federal Act on Data Protection (FADP)
perturbation.law_explanation: Art. 7 of the Swiss FADP mandates that those processing personal data must ensure data security. By explicitly stating the recipient isn't responsible for preventing inappropriate dissemination or use, the amended contract encourages violations of data protection principles, undermining the data controller's duty to protect personal information as required by law. The recipient has the duty to protect this data. By waiving this, it goes against the law.

perturbation.justification: The modified contract removes the recipient's responsibility for ensuring data is not misused, while the Law Explanation states that Article 7 of the Swiss FADP mandates those processing personal data must ensure data security. The altered text explicitly states the recipient is not responsible for preventing inappropriate dissemination or use, undermining the data controller's duty to protect personal information as required by law. This creates a direct contradiction, as the contract now seemingly encourages data protection violations, which the law aims to prevent.
